questions.question: Define logical implication and explain when p → q is false.
keyPoints: p → q means if p then q
It is false only when p is true and q is false
True in all other cases

questions.question: State and explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens with examples.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: (p → q) ∧ p ⇒ q
Example: If it rains, ground is wet. It rains. Therefore ground is wet
Modus Tollens: (p → q) ∧ ¬q ⇒ ¬p
Example: If it rains, ground is wet. Ground is not wet. Therefore it did not rain

questions.question: Explain the difference between valid and sound arguments.
keyPoints: Valid: truth of premises guarantees truth of conclusion
Sound: valid argument + all premises are actually true
A sound argument is always valid, but not vice versa

questions.question: What are the contrapositive, converse, and inverse of p → q? Explain their logical relationships.
keyPoints: Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p (logically equivalent to original)
Converse: q → p (not equivalent)
Inverse: ¬p → ¬q (equivalent to converse, not to original)

questions.question: Explain Hypothetical Syllogism and Disjunctive Syllogism with one example each.
keyPoints: Hypothetical Syllogism: [(p→q) ∧ (q→r)] ⇒ (p→r)
Example: If A then B, if B then C, therefore if A then C
Disjunctive Syllogism: [(p∨q) ∧ ¬p] ⇒ q
Example: It is red or blue. It is not red. Therefore it is blue

questions.question: List and state four rules of inference involving conjunction and disjunction.
keyPoints: Conjunction: p, q ⇒ (p ∧ q)
Simplification: (p ∧ q) ⇒ p
Addition: p ⇒ (p ∨ q)
Resolution: (p∨q) ∧ (¬q∨r) ⇒ (p∨r)

questions.question: Determine whether the following argument is valid: 'If the program compiles, then it has no syntax errors. The program has no syntax errors. Therefore, the program compiles.'
keyPoints: Identify the form: (p → q) ∧ q, therefore p
This is the fallacy of affirming the consequent
The argument is INVALID
Even if there are no syntax errors, program may not compile due to other reasons
Correct form would require p → q and p to conclude q (Modus Ponens)

questions.question: Use rules of inference to show that from premises: (1) If it rains, then the street is wet. (2) If the street is wet, then it is slippery. (3) It is not slippery. Prove that 'It did not rain'.
keyPoints: From (2) and (3) using Modus Tollens: Street wet → slippery, not slippery, therefore street is not wet
From (1) and result using Modus Tollens: Rains → street wet, street not wet, therefore did not rain
Or use Hypothetical Syllogism first to get: If it rains, then it is slippery
Then apply Modus Tollens with (3)

questions.question: What are logical fallacies? Explain 'affirming the consequent' and 'denying the antecedent' with examples.
keyPoints: Logical fallacy: invalid argument form that appears valid
Affirming consequent: [(p→q) ∧ q] ⇒ p - INVALID
Example: If hungry, then eat. Ate. Therefore hungry (false - ate for other reasons)
Denying antecedent: [(p→q) ∧ ¬p] ⇒ ¬q - INVALID
Example: If hungry, then eat. Not hungry. Therefore did not eat (false - ate for other reasons)

questions.question: Prove that p → q is logically equivalent to ¬p ∨ q using a truth table. Also state this equivalence.
keyPoints: p→q is false only when p=T and q=F
¬p∨q is false only when p=T and q=F
Both have identical truth values
Therefore p→q ≡ ¬p∨q (material implication)
This is the Law of Material Implication
